content:
Python orchestrates your agents. Rust executes them. Here’s why that split is now inevitable — and how to build on the right side of it.

8 min read
Just now

--

The Loop That Broke Production
You’ve shipped an AI agent. It works great in dev.
Then you hit scale.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

The future of AI agents is not Python or Rust. It is Python on top, Rust underneath.Your FastAPI orchestrator idles at 120MB of RAM before a single tool call fires. Cold starts clock in at 400ms. Three concurrent agents start hammering the GIL. Your vector DB is fine. Your LLM is fine. The problem is the runtime holding everything together.
This is the agent infrastructure tax. And in 2026, there’s a clean answer to it that almost nobody on Medium has written about yet.
The Rust agent runtime layer is here. It’s production-ready. It’s eating GitHub. And most Python-fluent AI engineers haven’t looked at it.
What Changed in 2026
Six months ago, if you wanted to build AI agents in Rust, you were largely on your own. The language had the primitives — tokio, reqwest, serde — but no coherent framework story for stitching them into autonomous loops.
